name: transactions
context: Run MongoDB multi-document ACID transactions with sessions, retry-aware logic, and explicit constraints
info: This skill covers session-backed transactions through the MongoDB Node.js driver: starting a session, using `withTransaction()` or the core transaction API, passing transaction options like read concern, write concern, read preference, and `maxCommitTimeMS`, and handling transient failures safely. Agents use it when a change must span multiple documents or collections atomically. It also calls out the important limits: transactions require a replica set or sharded cluster, carry overhead, and should not replace sound document design.
triggers: transfer data between mongodb collections atomically

sections.title: Session lifecycle
sections.body: Create a session with `client.startSession()`, run the transaction inside `withTransaction()` or with the core API, and end the session when done. All operations in the transaction must use the same session instance and the same `MongoClient` that created it.

sections.title: Transaction options and retries
sections.body: Transaction options let you override the client defaults for `readConcern`, `writeConcern`, `readPreference`, and `maxCommitTimeMS`. Design the work so a retry is safe, because transient transaction errors and commit-time uncertainty can require a second attempt. That usually means avoiding side effects outside MongoDB inside the transaction callback.

sections.title: Operational constraints
sections.body: Transactions carry overhead and should not become the default answer to every consistency problem. They are unsupported on standalone deployments, and parallel operations in a single transaction are not supported. When possible, restructure the data model so one atomic document update is enough.

links.context: Topology and connection settings determine whether transactions are even available
Use normal CRUD first and escalate only when multi-document atomicity is required
Transactions do not bypass collection validators

related.note: Sessions and transaction support depend on correct topology, URI options, and roles
Single-document atomic writes are cheaper and usually preferable when they satisfy the invariant

scripts.name: Run transaction
scripts.description: Execute a sequence of MongoDB write operations in a single session-backed transaction using Extended JSON input

TRANSACTION_INPUT_JSON.description: Extended JSON payload. If omitted, the script reads JSON from stdin.

database.description: Default database for operations unless overridden per step

operations.description: Ordered write operations to execute in the transaction

depends.reason: Transactions depend on topology, URI settings, and roles that allow session-backed writes
Transactions are composed from normal CRUD operations and should only be used when CRUD atomicity is insufficient
